title: Variational Embeddings for Community Detection and Node Representation

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - - VECODER VARIATIONAL EMBEDDINGS FOR COM MUNITY DETECTION AND NODE REPRESENTATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

In this paper, we study how to simultaneously learn two highly correlated tasks of graph analysis, i.e., community detection and node representation learning. We propose an efficient generative model called **VECODER** for jointly learning **V** ariational **E** mbeddings for **Co** mmunity **De** tection and node **R** epresentation. VECODER assumes that every node can be a member of one or more communities. The node embeddings are learned in such a way that connected nodes are not only “closer” to each other but also share similar community assignments. A joint learning framework leverages community-aware node embeddings for better community detection. We demonstrate on several graph datasets that VECODER effectively outperforms many competitive baselines on all three tasks i.e. node classification, overlapping community detection and non-overlapping community detection. We also show that VECODER is computationally efficient and has quite robust performance with varying hyperparameters.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Graphs are flexible data structures that model complex relationships among entities, i.e. data points as nodes and the relations between nodes via edges. One important task in graph analysis is community detection, where the objective is to cluster nodes into multiple groups (communities). Each community is a set of densely connected nodes. The communities can be overlapping or non-overlapping, depending on whether they share some nodes or not. Several algorithmic (Ahn et al., 2010; Der´enyi et al., 2005) and probabilistic approaches (Gopalan & Blei, 2013; Leskovec & Mcauley, 2012; Wang et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2013) to community detection have been proposed. Another fundamental task in graph analysis is learning the node embeddings. These embeddings can then be used for downstream tasks like graph visualization (Tang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016; Gao et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2017) and classification (Cao et al., 2015; Tang et al., 2015).

In the literature, these tasks are usually treated separately. Although the standard graph embedding methods capture the basic connectivity, the learning of the node embeddings is independent of community detection. For instance, a simple approach can be to get the node embeddings via DeepWalk (Perozzi et al., 2014) and get community assignments for each node by using k-means or Gaussian mixture model. Looking from the other perspective, methods like Bigclam (Yang & Leskovec (2013)), that focus on finding the community structure in the dataset, perform poorly for node-representation tasks e.g. node classification. This motivates us to study the approaches that jointly learn community-aware node embeddings.

Recently several approaches, like CNRL (Tu et al., 2018), ComE (Cavallari et al., 2017), vGraph (Sun et al. (2019)) etc, have been proposed to learn the node embeddings and detect communities simultaneously in a unified framework. Several studies have shown that community detection is improved by incorporating the node representation in the learning process (Cao et al., 2015; Kozdoba & Mannor, 2015). The intuition is that the global structure of graphs learned during community detection can provide useful context for node embeddings and vice versa.

The joint learning methods (CNRL, ComE and vGraph) learn two embeddings for each node. One node embedding is used for the node representation task. The second node embedding is the “context” embedding of the node which aids in community detection. As CNRL and ComE are based on Skip-Gram (Mikolov et al., 2013) and DeepWalk (Perozzi et al., 2014), they inherit “context” embedding from it for learning the neighbourhood information of the node. vGraph also requires two

1

node embeddings for parameterizing two different distributions. In contrast, we propose learning a single community-aware node representation which is directly used for both tasks. In this way, we not only get rid of an extraneous node embedding but also reduce the computational cost.

In this paper, we propose an efficient generative model called **VECODER** for jointly learning both community detection and node representation. The underlying intuition behind VECODER is that every node can be a member of one or more communities. However, the node embeddings should be learned in such a way that connected nodes are “closer” to each other than unconnected nodes. Moreover, connected nodes should have similar community assignments. Formally, we assume that for _i_ -th node, the node embeddings _**z** i_ are generated from a prior distribution _p_ ( _**z**_ ). Given _**z** i_ , the community assignments _ci_ are sampled from _p_ ( _ci|_ _**z** i_ ), which is parameterized by node and community embeddings. In order to generate an edge ( _i, j_ ), we sample another node embedding _**z** j_ from _p_ ( _**z**_ ) and respective community assignment _cj_ from _p_ ( _cj|_ _**z** j_ ). Afterwards, the node embeddings and the respective community assignments of node pairs are fed to a decoder. The decoder ensures that embeddings of both the nodes and the communities of connected nodes share high similarity. This enables learning such node embeddings that are useful for both community detection and node representation tasks.

We validate the effectiveness of our approach on several real-world graph datasets. In Sec. 4, we show empirically that VECODER is able to outperform the baseline methods including the direct competitors on all three tasks i.e. node classification, overlapping community detection and nonoverlapping community detection. Furthermore, we compare the computational cost of training different algorithms. VECODER is up to 40x more time-efficient than its competitors. We also conduct hyperparameter sensitivity analysis which demonstrates the robustness of our approach. Our main contributions are summarized below:
- We propose an efficient generative model called **VECODER** for joint community detection and node representation learning.
- We adopt a novel approach and argue that a single node embedding is sufficient for learning both the representation of the node itself and its context.

- Training VECODER is extremely time-efficient in comparison to its competitors.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Community Detection.** Early community detection algorithms are inspired from clustering algorithms (Xie et al., 2013). For instance, spectral clustering (Tang & Liu, 2011) is applied to the graph Laplacian matrix for extracting the communities. Similarly, several matrix factorization based methods have been proposed to tackle the community detection problem. For example, Bigclam (Yang & Leskovec (2013)) treats the problem as a non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) task. It aims to recover the node-community affiliation matrix and learns the latent factors which represent community affiliations of nodes. Another method CESNA(Yang et al. (2013)) extends Bigclam by modelling the interaction between the network structure and the node attributes. The performance of matrix factorization methods is limited due to the capacity of the bi-linear models. Some generative models, like vGraph (Sun et al., 2019), Circles (Leskovec & Mcauley (2012)) etc, have also been proposed to detect communities in a graph.

**Node Representation Learning.** Many successful algorithms which learn node representation in an unsupervised way are based on random walk objectives (Perozzi et al., 2014; Tang et al., 2015; Grover & Leskovec, 2016; Hamilton et al., 2017). Some known issues with random-walk based methods (e.g. DeepWalk, node2vec etc) are: (1) They sacrifice the structural information of the graph by putting over-emphasis on the proximity information (Ribeiro et al., 2017) and (2) great dependence of the performance on hyperparameters (walk-length, number of hops etc) (Perozzi et al., 2014; Grover & Leskovec, 2016). Recently, Gilmer et al. (2017) recently showed that graph convolutions encoder models greatly reduce the need for using the random-walk based training objectives. This is because the graph convolutions enforce that the neighboring nodes have similar representations. Some interesting GCN based approaches include graph autoencoders e.g. GAE and VGAE(Kipf & Welling (2016b)) and DGI(Velickovic et al., 2019).
**Joint community detection and node representation learning.** In the literature, several attempts have been made to tackle both these tasks in a single framework. Most of these methods propose

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

an alternate optimization process, i.e. learn node embeddings and improve community assignments with them and vice versa (Cavallari et al., 2017; Tu et al., 2018). Some approaches, like CNRL (Tu et al., 2018) and ComE (Cavallari et al., 2017), are inspired from random walk, thus inheriting the shortcomings of random walk. Others, like GEMSEC (Rozemberczki et al. (2019), are limited to the detection of non-overlapping communities. There also exist some generative models like CommunityGAN (Jia et al. (2019)) and vGraph (Sun et al. (2019)) that jointly learn community assignments and node embeddings. Some methods have high computational complexity, i.e. quadratic to the number of nodes in a graph, e.g. M-NMF (Wang et al. (2017)) and DNR (Yang et al., 2016a). CNRL, ComE and vGraph require learning two embeddings for each node for simultaneously tackling the two tasks. Unlike them, VECODER learns a single community-aware node representation which is directly used for both tasks.

It is pertinent to highlight that although both vGraph and VECODER adopt a variational approach but the underlying models are quite different. vGraph assumes that each node can be represented as a mixture of multiple communities and is described by a multinomial distribution over communities, whereas VECODER models the node embedding by a single distribution. For a given node, vGraph, first draws a community assignment and then a connected neighbor node is generated based on the assignment. Whereas, VECODER draws the node embedding from prior distribution and then community assignment is conditioned on a single node only. In simple terms, vGraph also needs edge information in the generative process whereas VECODER does not require it. VECODER relies on the decoder to ensure that embeddings of the connected nodes and their communities share high similarity with each other.

## 3 METHODOLOGY

## 3.1 PROBLEM FORMULATION

Suppose an undirected graph _G_ = ( _V, E_ ) with the adjacency matrix _**A** ∈_ R _[N][×][N]_ and a matrix _**X** ∈_ R _[N][×][F]_ of _F_ -dimensional node features, _N_ being the number of nodes. Given _K_ as the number of communities, we aim to jointly learn the node embeddings and the community embeddings following a variational approach such that: (1) One or more communities can be assigned to every node and (2) the node embeddings can be used for both community detection and node classification.

For better reconstructing the edges, Eq. (12) makes use of the community embeddings, node embeddings and community assignment information simultaneously. This helps in learning better node representations by leveraging the global information about the graph structure via community detection. On the other hand, this also forces the community assignment information to exploit the local graph structure via node embeddings and edge information.

## 3.4 PRACTICAL ASPECTS

The third term in Eq. (7) is estimated in practice using the samples generated by the approximate posterior. This term is equivalent to the negative of binary cross-entropy (BCE) loss between observed edges and reconstructed edges. Since community assignment follows a categorical distribution, we use Gumbel-softmax (Jang et al. (2016)) for backpropagation of the gradients. As for the second term of Eq. (7), it is also enough to set _M_ = 1, i.e. use only one sample per input node.

## 3.5 COMPLEXITY

Computation of dot products for all combinations of node and community embeddings takes _O_ ( _NKd_ ) time. Solving Eq. (11) further requires calculation of mean of dot products over the neighborhood for every node, which takes _O_ ( _|E|K_ ) computations overall as we traverse every edge for every community. Finally, we need softmax over all communities for every node in Eq. (10) and Eq. (11) which takes _O_ ( _NK_ ) time. Eq. (12) takes _O_ ( _|E|_ ) time for all edges as we have already calculated the dot products. As a result, the overall complexity becomes _O_ ( _|E|K_ + _NKd_ ). This complexity is quite low compared to other algorithms designed to achieve similar goals (Cavallari et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2016a).

We have selected 18 different datasets ranging from 270 to 126,842 edges. For nonoverlapping community detection and node classification, we use 5 the citation datasets (Bojchevski & G¨unnemann (2017); Yang et al. (2016b)). The remaining datasets (Leskovec & Mcauley (2012); Yang & Leskovec (2015)), used for overlapping community detection, are taken from SNAP repository (Leskovec & Krevl (2014)). Following (Sun et al., 2019), we take 5 biggest ground truth communities for youtube, amazon and dblp. Moreover, we also analyse the case of large number of communities. ## 4.2 BASELINES

For overlapping community detection, we compare with the following competitive baselines: **MNMF** (Wang et al., 2017) learns community membership distribution by using joint non-negative matrix factorization with modularity based regularization. **BIGCLAM** (Yang & Leskovec (2013)) also formulates community detection as a non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) task. It simultaneously optimizes the model likelihood of observed links and learns the latent factors which represent community affiliations of nodes. **CESNA** (Yang et al. (2013)) extends BIGCLAM by statistically modelling the interaction between the network structure and the node attributes. **Circles** (Leskovec & Mcauley (2012)) introduces a generative model for community detection in ego-networks by learning node similarity metrics for every community. **SVI** (Gopalan & Blei (2013)) formulates membership of nodes in multiple communities by a Bayesian model of networks. **vGraph** (Sun et al. (2019)) simultaneously learns node embeddings and community assignments by modelling the nodes as being generated from a mixture of communities. **vGraph+** , a variant further incorporates regularization to weigh local connectivity. **ComE** (Cavallari et al. (2017)) jointly learns community and node embeddings by using gaussian mixture model formulation. **CNRL** (Tu et al., 2018) enhances the random walk sequences (generated by DeepWalk, node2vec etc) to jointly learn community and node embeddings. **CommunityGAN** (ComGAN)is a generative adversarial model for learning node embeddings such that the entries of the embedding vector of each node refer to the membership strength of the node to different communities. Lastly, we compare the results with the communities obtained by applying k-means to the learned embeddings of **DGI** (Velickovic et al., 2019).

For non-overlapping community detection and node classification, in addition to MNMF, DGI, CNRL, CommunityGAN, vGraph and ComE, we compare VECODER with the following baselines: **DeepWalk** (Perozzi et al. (2014)) makes use of SkipGram (Mikolov et al. (2013)) and truncated random walks on network to learn node embeddings. **LINE** (Tang et al. (2015)) learns node embeddings while attempting to preserve first and second order proximities of nodes. **Node2Vec** (Grover & Leskovec (2016)) learns the embeddings using biased random walk while aiming to preserve network neighborhoods of nodes. **Graph Autoencoder (GAE)** Kipf & Welling (2016b) extends the idea of autoencoders to graph datasets. We also include its variational counterpart i.e. **VGAE** . **GEMSEC** is a sequence sampling-based learning model which aims to jointly learn the node embeddings and clustering assignments.

## 4.3 SETTINGS

**For overlapping community detection** , we learn mean and log-variance matrices of 16dimensional node embeddings. We set _α_ = 0 _._ 9 and _ϵ_ = 0 _._ 3 in all our experiments. Following Kipf & Welling (2016b), we first pre-train a variational graph autoencoder. We perform gradient descent with Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba (2014)) and learning rate = 0 _._ 01. Community assignments are obtained using Eq. (14). For the baselines, we employ the results reported by Sun et al. (2019). For evaluating the performance, we use _F1-score_ and _Jaccard similarity_ .

**For non-overlapping community detection** , since the default implementations of most the baselines use 128 dimensional embeddings, for we use _d_ = 128 for fair comparison. Eq. (13) is used for community assignments. For vGraph, we use the code provided by the authors. We employ _normalized mutual information (NMI)_ and _adjusted random index (ARI)_ as evaluation metrics.

**For node classification** , we follow the training split used in various previous works (Yang et al., 2016b; Kipf & Welling, 2016a; Velickovic et al., 2019), i.e. 20 nodes per class for training. We train logistic regression using LIBLINEAR (Fan et al. (2008)) solver as our classifier and report the evaluation results on rest of the nodes. For the algorithms that do not use node features, we train the classifier by appending the raw node features with the learnt embeddings. For evaluation, we use _F1-macro_ and _F1-micro_ scores.
All the reported results are the average over five runs. Further implementation details can be found in the code: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/1d95bf8f-8ce3-4870-a454-07db463b419f.

## 4.4 DISCUSSION OF RESULTS

In the following, we discuss the results to gain some important insights into the problem.

Tables 2 and 3 summarize the results of the performance comparison for the overlapping community detection task.

First, we note that our proposed method VECODER outperforms the competitive methods on all datasets in terms of Jaccard Similarity. VECODER also outperforms its competitors on 12 out of 13 datasets in terms of F1-score. It is the second best method on the 13th dataset ( _fb0_ ). These results demonstrate the capability of VECODER to learn multiple community assignments quite well and hence reinforces our intuition behind the design of Eq. (11).

Second, we observe that there is no consistent performing algorithm among the competitive methods. That is, excluding VECODER , the best performance is achieved by vGraph/vGraph+ on 5, ComGAN on 4 and ComE on 3 out of 13 datasets in terms of F1-score. A a similar trend can be seen in Jaccard Similarity. Third, it is noted that all the methods which achieve second best performance are solving the task of community detection and node representation learning jointly. This supports our claim that treating the two tasks jointly results in better performance.

Fourth, we observe that vGraph+ results are generally better than vGraph. This is because vGraph+ incorporates a regularization term in the loss function which is based on Jaccard coefficients of connected nodes as edge weights. However, it should be noted that this prepossessing step is computationally expensive for densely connected graphs.

Tab. 4 shows the results on non-overlapping community detection. First, we observe that MNMF, DeepWalk, LINE and Node2Vec provide a good baseline for the task. However, these methods are not able to achieve comparable performance on any dataset relative to the frameworks that treat the two tasks jointly. Second, VECODER consistently outperforms all the competitors in NMI and ARI metrics, except for _CiteSeer_ where it achieves second best ARI. Third, we observe that GCN based models i.e. GAE, VGAE and DGI show competitive performance. That is, they achieve second best performance in all the datasets except _CiteSeer_ . In particular, DGI achieves second best NMI results in 3 out of 5 datasets and 2 out of 5 datsets in terms of ARI. Nonetheless, DGI results are not very competitive in Tab. 2 and Tab. 3, showing that while DGI can be a good choice for learning node embeddings for attributed graphs with non-overlapping communities, it is not the best option for non-attributed graphs or overlapping communities.

The results for node classification are presented in Tab. 5. VECODER achieves best F1-micro and F1-macro scores on 4 out of 5 datasets. We also observe that GCN based models i.e. GAE, VGAE and DGI show competitive performance, following the trend in results of Tab. 4. Furthermore, we note that the node classification results of CommunityGan (ComGAN) are quite poor. We think a potential reason behind it is that the node embeddings are constrained to have same dimensions as the number of communities. Hence, different components of the learned node embeddings simply represent the membership strengths of nodes for different communities. The linear classifiers may find it difficult to separate such vectors.

## 4.5 HYPERPARAMETER SENSITIVITY

We study the dependence of VECODER on _ϵ_ and _α_ by evaluating on four datasets of different sizes: _fb698_ ( _N_ = 61), _fb1912_ ( _N_ = 747), _amazon1000_ (N=1540) and _youtube_ ( _N_ = 5346). We sweep for _ϵ_ = _{_ 0 _._ 1 _,_ 0 _._ 2 _, · · · ,_ 0 _._ 9 _}_ . For demonstrating effect of _α_ , we fix _ϵ_ = 0 _._ 3 and sweep for _α_ = _{_ 0 _._ 1 _,_ 0 _._ 2 _, · · · ,_ 0 _._ 9 _}_ . The average results of five runs for _ϵ_ and _α_ are given in Fig. 1a and Fig. 1b respectively. Overall VECODER is quite robust to the change in the values of _ϵ_ and _α_ . In case of _ϵ_ , we see a general trend of decrease in performance when the threshold _ϵ_ is set quite high e.g. _ϵ >_ 0 _._ 7. This is because the datasets contain overlapping communities and a very high _ϵ_ will cause the algorithm to give only the most probable community assignment instead of potentially providing multiple communities per node. However, for a large part of sweep space, the results are almost consistent. When _ϵ_ is fixed and _α_ is changed, the results are mostly consistent except when _α_ is set to a low value. Eq. (11) shows that in such a case the node itself is almost neglected and VECODER tends to assign communities based upon neighborhood only, which may cause a decrease in the performance. This effect is most visible in _amazon1000_ dataset because it has only 1.54 points on average per community i.e. there is a good chance for neighbours of a point of being in different communities. Therefore, only depending upon the neighbors will most likely result in poor results.

## 4.6 TRAINING TIME

Now we compare the training times of different algorithms in Fig. 2. As some of the baselines are more resource intensive than others, we select aws instance type g4dn.4xlarge for fair comparison of training times. For vGraph, we train for 1000 iterations and for VECODER for 1500 iterations. For all other algorithms we use the default parameters as used in section 4.3. We observe that the methods that simply output the node embeddings take relatively less time compared to the algorithms that jointly learn node representations and community assignments e.g VECODER , vGraph and CNRL. Among these algorithms VECODER is the most time efficient. It consistently trains in less time compared to its direct competitors. For instance, it is about 12 times faster than ComE for _CiteSeer-full_ and about 40 times faster compared to vGraph for _Cora-full_ dataset. This provides evidence for lower computational complexity of VECODER in Section 3.5.

## 5 CONCLUSION

We propose a scalable generative method VECODER to simultaneously perform community detection and node representation learning. Our novel approach learns a single community-aware node embedding for both the representation of the node and its context. VECODER is scalable due to its low complexity, i.e. _O_ ( _|E|K_ + _NKd_ ). The experiments on several graph datasets show that VECODER consistently outperforms all the competitive baselines on node classification, overlapping community detection and non-overlapping community detection tasks. Moreover, training the VECODER is highly time-efficient than its competitors.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes VECoDeR, a variational model for joint community detection and node representation learning. While the method shows strong empirical performance and computational efficiency, several critical issues undermine its contribution and completeness. The most significant concerns are the lack of methodological novelty compared to vGraph, missing statistical validation of performance claims, insufficient ablation studies to justify the joint learning framework, and potential scalability issues for large graphs.

The paper presents a well-executed empirical study with comprehensive experiments across multiple tasks and datasets. The computational efficiency claims are well-supported, and the hyperparameter sensitivity analysis is thorough. However, the methodological contribution appears incremental rather than novel, the statistical significance of results is not established, and key questions about the necessity of joint learning remain unanswered.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient methodological novelty over vGraph: The paper claims VECoDeR's novelty lies in using a single embedding for both tasks, but Section 3.2 reveals the model architecture is remarkably similar to vGraph. Both use variational inference with node embeddings generating community assignments. The key difference claimed is that VECoDeR doesn't require edge information in the generative process, yet Eq. (12) shows the decoder explicitly uses edge information through pθ(aij|...). The paper needs to more clearly articulate how this architectural difference leads to fundamentally different capabilities or performance, rather than just being a minor variant of vGraph's framework.

- Missing statistical significance testing: The paper reports performance improvements across all tasks but provides no statistical significance testing. With results averaged over only 5 runs (Section 4.3), and performance differences often within 5-10% (Tables 2-5), readers cannot determine if these improvements are statistically meaningful. For a paper claiming consistent superiority across 18 datasets, statistical validation is essential. The revision should include p-values or confidence intervals for the performance differences, particularly against the strongest competitors (ComE, vGraph+, DGI).

- No ablation study for joint learning contribution: The paper's central claim is that joint learning improves both tasks, but no ablation study demonstrates this. A critical missing experiment is comparing VECoDeR against: (1) using only the node embeddings for representation learning without community detection, and (2) using only community assignments without the embedding constraint. Without this, readers cannot assess whether the claimed benefits of joint learning justify the added complexity. Section 4.4 discusses results but never isolates the contribution of the joint framework itself.

- Scalability analysis incomplete for large graphs: While Section 3.5 claims O(|E|K + NKd) complexity, the largest dataset tested has only 126,842 edges (Cora-full). For modern graph ML methods, scalability to millions of edges is expected. The computational efficiency claims (up to 40x faster) are only demonstrated on relatively small graphs. The paper should include experiments on larger graphs (e.g., >1M edges) or provide theoretical analysis of memory requirements, as the O(|E|K) term could become prohibitive when K is large (as shown with amazon1000).

- Hyperparameter settings lack justification: The paper fixes α=0.9 and ε=0.3 across all experiments without justification. Section 4.5 shows performance varies significantly with these parameters, yet no principled method for selecting them is provided. For α values below 0.3, performance degrades substantially, suggesting the model is sensitive to this hyperparameter. The paper should either: (1) provide a method for automatically selecting these values per dataset, or (2) demonstrate that the chosen values work across a broader range of graph characteristics.

- Missing synthetic validation of model assumptions: The paper claims VECoDeR's design ensures connected nodes have similar community assignments, but never demonstrates this property on synthetic data where ground truth is known. A standard validation would be to generate graphs from the VECoDeR generative process itself (or from a stochastic block model with known communities) and verify that the model can recover the true community structure. This would show the model's assumptions are internally consistent and that the ELBO optimization actually learns the intended structure. Without this, readers cannot assess whether the model is learning meaningful communities or just exploiting heuristics that happen to work on the benchmark datasets.

- No worked example of ELBO computation: The paper presents the ELBO in Equation (7) but never shows how it is computed for a concrete graph structure. Readers would benefit from a small, fully-specified example (e.g., a 5-node graph with known edges) showing the step-by-step computation of each ELBO term: the KL divergences, the sampling process, and the reconstruction term. This is particularly important because the paper claims computational efficiency advantages, but practitioners cannot verify these claims without understanding the actual computational steps involved in a single forward/backward pass.

- Missing guidance on community number selection: The paper assumes the number of communities K is given as input, but provides no discussion of how to select K in practice. This is a critical limitation since real-world graphs rarely come with pre-specified community counts. The paper should either: (1) demonstrate performance sensitivity to different K values on the benchmark datasets, (2) propose a method for automatically selecting K (e.g., using ELBO-based model selection), or (3) show how the model performs when K is over- or under-specified. Without this guidance, practitioners cannot apply VECoDeR to new graphs where K is unknown.

- No comparison with separate task baselines: The paper claims joint learning is superior but never compares against the natural baseline of running separate state-of-the-art methods for each task. For example, a strong baseline would be: (1) learn node embeddings with DGI/VGAE, then (2) run Bigclam on the learned embeddings for community detection. Similarly, for node classification, compare against using embeddings from a dedicated representation learning method without any community detection component. Without these comparisons, the claimed benefits of joint learning remain unproven—it's possible the improvements come from VECoDeR's specific architecture rather than the joint learning framework itself.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add statistical significance testing for all reported performance improvements with proper multiple comparison correction

- Include ablation study comparing joint learning against separate task learning to validate the core contribution

- Provide clearer differentiation from vGraph including architectural diagrams and theoretical analysis of differences

- Test scalability on larger graphs (>1M edges) or provide memory complexity analysis for real-world deployment

- Develop principled hyperparameter selection method or demonstrate broader robustness across different α, ε settings

comments.title: Edge decoder violates symmetry requirement
comments.explanation: Equation (12) is not symmetric under swapping nodes i and j when ℓ ≠ m. Since edge probability should be symmetric for undirected graphs, this formulation is problematic for cross-community edges. Rewrite Equation (12) as $p_{\theta}(a_{ij}|c_{i},c_{j},\bm{z}_{i},\bm{z}_{j}) = \sigma(\frac{<\bm{z}_{i},\bm{g}_{c_{j}}> + <\bm{z}_{j},\bm{g}_{c_{i}}>}{2})$ to ensure symmetry.

comments.title: Undefined neighborhood term for isolated nodes
comments.quote: $q_{\phi}(c_{i}=k|\bm{z}_{i},\mathcal{G})=\frac{\text{exp}\Big{(}\alpha<\bm{z}_{i},\bm{g}_{k}>+(1-\alpha)\frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_{i}|}\sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}_{i}}<\bm{z}_{j},\bm{g}_{k}>\Big{)}}{\sum_{\ell=1}^{K}\text{exp}\Big{(}\alpha<\bm{z}_{i},\bm{g}_{\ell}>+(1-\alpha)\frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_{i}|}\sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}_{i}}<\bm{z}_{j},\bm{g}_{\ell}>\Big{)}}.$ (11)
comments.explanation: The term $\frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_{i}|}$ causes division by zero when node i has no neighbors. This makes the model undefined for isolated nodes. Add after Equation (11): 'For isolated nodes where $|\mathcal{N}_{i}| = 0$, we define the neighborhood term as 0, effectively setting $\alpha = 1$ for such nodes.' Alternatively, modify Equation (11) to use $\max(1, |\mathcal{N}_{i}|)$ in the denominator.

comments.title: Gumbel-softmax mismatch with overlapping communities
comments.quote: Since community assignment follows a categorical distribution, we use Gumbel-softmax (Jang et al. (2016)) for backpropagation of the gradients.
comments.explanation: The paper claims community assignment follows a categorical distribution and uses Gumbel-softmax for gradient backpropagation. However, Equation (14) shows overlapping community assignments are obtained by thresholding probabilities, which implies multi-label assignments where nodes can belong to multiple communities. Gumbel-softmax is designed for categorical distributions where exactly one category is selected. This creates a mismatch between the claimed distribution and the actual model behavior. Rewrite the sentence to clarify that Gumbel-softmax is used for the non-overlapping case while independent binary decisions are used for overlapping communities.

comments.title: Unfair training iteration comparison
comments.quote: For vGraph, we train for 1000 iterations and for VECODER for 1500 iterations. For all other algorithms we use the default parameters as used in section 4.3.
comments.explanation: The training time comparison is not fair because VECODER is trained for 50% more iterations than vGraph (1500 vs 1000). Since training time scales linearly with iterations, this difference inflates VECODER's apparent time advantage. To make a valid comparison, all methods should be trained for the same number of iterations or until convergence. The 12x and 40x speedup claims may be partially due to this iteration mismatch rather than inherent algorithmic efficiency.

comments.title: Missing sampling process definition
comments.quote: {z}_{i}))-\sum_{i=1}^{N}\frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M}D_{KL}(q_{\phi}(c_{i}|\bm{z}_{i}^{(m)},\mathcal{I})\mid\mid p_{\theta}(c_{i}|\bm{z}_{i}^{(m)}))$
comments.explanation: The notation $\bm{z}_{i}^{(m)}$ lacks definition. This appears to be Monte Carlo sampling where $\bm{z}_{i}^{(m)} \sim q_{\phi}(\bm{z}_{i}|\mathcal{I})$. Add clarification after Eq. (7): 'where $\bm{z}_{i}^{(m)}$ are M samples drawn from the approximate posterior $q_{\phi}(\bm{z}_{i}|\mathcal{I})$ using Monte Carlo estimation.'

comments.title: Ambiguous community assignment representation
comments.quote: Let us denote the latent node embedding and community assignment for $i$-th node by the random variables $\bm{z}_{i}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}$ and $c_{i}$ respectively.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims VECoDeR handles overlapping communities, but $c_{i}$ is not defined to support multi-community membership. For overlapping communities, $c_{i}$ should be a vector or multi-hot encoding, not a scalar. Clarify whether $c_{i} \in \{1,2,...,K\}$ (non-overlapping) or $c_{i} \in \{0,1\}^K$ (overlapping) to match the abstract's claims.

comments.title: M=1 sampling assumption not justified
comments.quote: As for the second term of Eq. (7), it is also enough to set  $M = 1$ , i.e. use only one sample per input node.
comments.explanation: The paper claims it is 'enough' to set M=1 for the second term of Eq. (7) without providing justification. In variational inference, using a single sample can lead to high variance gradient estimates. The authors should either cite a specific result showing that M=1 suffices in their setting, or provide empirical evidence that increasing M does not improve performance.

comments.title: Missing NK term in complexity analysis
comments.quote: Finally, we need softmax over all communities for every node in Eq. (10) and Eq. (11) which takes  $\mathcal{O}(NK)$  time. Eq. (12) takes  $\mathcal{O}(|\mathcal{E}|)$  time for all edges as we have already calculated the dot products. As a result, the overall complexity becomes  $\mathcal{O}(|\mathcal{E}|K + NKd)$ .
comments.explanation: The complexity analysis correctly identifies an $\mathcal{O}(NK)$ term for softmax computations but then omits it from the final complexity expression. The complete complexity should be $\mathcal{O}(|\mathcal{E}|K + NKd + NK)$. While $\mathcal{O}(NK)$ is dominated by $\mathcal{O}(NKd)$ when $d \geq 2$, this assumption should be explicitly stated. Rewrite the final sentence as 'As a result, the overall complexity becomes $\mathcal{O}(|\mathcal{E}|K + NKd + NK)$, which simplifies to $\mathcal{O}(|\mathcal{E}|K + NKd)$ when $d \geq 2$'.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in approximate posterior
comments.quote:
The corresponding approximate posterior $q_{\phi}(\bm{z}_{i}|\mathcal{I})$ in Eq. (6), used as node embeddings encoder, is given by

$q_{\phi}(\bm{z}_{i}|\mathcal{I})=\mathcal{N}\big{(}\bm{\mu}_{i}(\mathcal{I}),\text{diag}(\bm{\sigma}^{2}{}_{i}(\mathcal{I}))\big{)}.$ (9)
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathcal{I}$ in Equation (9) is undefined in the paper. While Equation (11) conditions on $\mathcal{G}$, the text doesn't clarify whether $\mathcal{I}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ refer to the same information. This creates ambiguity about what the encoder network receives as input. Rewrite Equation (9) as $q_{\phi}(\bm{z}_{i}|\mathcal{G})=\mathcal{N}(\bm{\mu}_{i}(\mathcal{G}),\text{diag}(\bm{\sigma}^{2}{}_{i}(\mathcal{G})))$ and add a sentence before Equation (9) stating: 'Here $\mathcal{G}=(V,E,X)$ represents the graph structure including node features $X$ if available.'

comments.title: Contradiction between robustness claim and observed sensitivity
comments.quote: Overall VECoDeR is quite robust to the change in the values of $\epsilon$ and $\alpha$. In case of $\epsilon$, we see a general trend of decrease in performance when the threshold $\epsilon$ is set quite high e.g. $\epsilon>0.7$.
comments.explanation: The text claims VECoDeR is 'quite robust' but then describes significant performance degradation for $\epsilon>0.7$, which represents 30% of the parameter space tested. Similarly, it mentions performance decreases for low $\alpha$ values. This contradicts the robustness claim. The authors should qualify the robustness claim or acknowledge the sensitivity. Rewrite as: 'Overall VECoDeR shows reasonable stability across moderate values of $\epsilon$ and $\alpha$, though performance degrades significantly for extreme values ($\epsilon>0.7$ and $\alpha<0.3$).'

comments.title: Inaccurate claim about GEMSEC limitations
comments.quote: Others, like GEMSEC (*Rozemberczki et al. (2019)*, are limited to the detection of non-overlapping communities.
comments.explanation: This claim is factually incorrect. GEMSEC (Rozemberczki et al., 2019) explicitly supports overlapping community detection through its soft clustering mechanism. The method learns soft community assignments where nodes can belong to multiple communities simultaneously. The paper should correct this statement.

comments.title: Mean field approximation needs justification
comments.quote:
The distribution $q_{\phi}(\bm{z}_{i},\bm{z}_{j},c_{i},c_{j}|\mathcal{I})$ in the third term of Eq. (7) is factorized into two conditionally independent distributions i.e.

$q_{\phi}(\bm{z}_{i},\bm{z}_{j},c_{i},c_{j}|\mathcal{I})=q_{\phi}(\bm{z}_{i},c_{i}|\mathcal{I})q_{\phi}(\bm{z}_{j},c_{j}|\mathcal{I}).$
comments.explanation: While this is a mean field approximation, it deserves more justification given that edges create dependencies between nodes. Add after Eq. (8): 'We employ a mean field approximation assuming conditional independence between node pairs in the posterior. This approximation, while ignoring edge-induced dependencies, enables tractable inference and is commonly used in variational graph models.'

comments.title: Unsubstantiated performance claim
comments.quote: In Sec. 4, we show empirically that VECoDeR is able to outperform the baseline methods including the direct competitors on all three tasks i.e. node classification, overlapping community detection and non-overlapping community detection.
comments.explanation: The claim of outperforming baselines on 'all three tasks' is too strong given the actual results in Section 4. Tables 2-5 show that VECoDeR does not achieve the best performance on every metric across all datasets. The introduction should be revised to reflect that VECoDeR achieves competitive or superior performance overall, not universal superiority across all tasks and datasets.

comments.title: Thresholding formula may produce empty sets
comments.explanation: The thresholding formula in Equation (14) can produce empty community sets when ε > 1, which contradicts the stated range ε ∈ [0,1]. More importantly, when ε is close to 1 (e.g., ε = 0.9 as used in experiments), nodes may end up with no community assignments if their secondary community probabilities are all below the threshold relative to the maximum. Add 'Note that when ε = 1, the set always contains at least the community with maximum probability, and for ε < 1, additional communities may be included' after Equation (14).

comments.title: Inconsistent dataset size notation
comments.quote: We study the dependence of VECoDeR on $\epsilon$ and $\alpha$ by evaluating on four datasets of different sizes: fb698($N=61$), fb1912($N=747$), amazon1000(N=$1540$) and youtube($N=5346$).
comments.explanation: The notation for dataset sizes is inconsistent. fb698, fb1912, and amazon1000 use parentheses with $N=...$ while amazon1000 uses N=$1540$ without parentheses. More importantly, the values (61, 747, 1540, 5346) seem unusually small for graphs with these names. fb698 typically refers to Facebook networks with 698 nodes, not 61. This appears to be a transcription error. Rewrite the sentence with the correct node counts.

comments.title: Inconsistent model naming
comments.quote: We propose a scalable generative method VECODER to simultaneously perform community detection and node representation learning.
comments.explanation: The conclusion uses 'VECODER' throughout, but the paper title and abstract consistently use 'VECoDeR' (with capital C, D, and R). This naming inconsistency creates confusion. The conclusion should use the same naming convention as the rest of the paper.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes VECODER, a generative model for joint community detection and node representation learning, building on variational graph autoencoders and probabilistic community models. It presents a clean formulation, claims improved efficiency and performance over baselines including vGraph and ComE, and reports strong empirical results across multiple datasets and tasks. The core idea—using a single community-aware embedding instead of two separate ones—is conceptually appealing and aligns with recent trends in unified representation learning.

Overview issues:

- Lack of ablation study undermines claim of mutual improvement: The paper claims that community detection and node representation benefit from joint learning, but provides no ablation showing what happens when either component is removed. Without a version of VECODER that uses fixed communities to learn embeddings, or fixed embeddings to detect communities, it's unclear whether the joint framework actually enables mutual reinforcement or if performance gains come from other design choices like the encoder or decoder structure. This weakens the central argument for joint modeling, especially given that baselines like DGI already learn powerful embeddings without explicit community modules.

- Decoder formulation (Eq. 12) lacks theoretical and empirical justification: The edge decoder in Equation (12) combines node and community embeddings via averaging of sigmoid-transformed dot products, but this form is not derived from a probabilistic model or compared to alternatives. It is unclear why this specific combination should model edge likelihood better than standard inner-product decoders used in VGAE. The absence of a baseline using a conventional decoder (e.g., $\sigma(\bm{z}_i^\top \bm{z}_j)$) makes it impossible to assess the contribution of the proposed community-aware decoder. Moreover, the use of community assignments $c_i, c_j$ in the decoder while conditioning on them in the prior suggests a mismatch between generative assumptions and inference.

- Community assignment posterior (Eq. 11) introduces neighborhood bias without validation: Equation (11) blends a node's own embedding similarity with its neighbors' average similarity using hyperparameter $\alpha$, claiming this improves community assignment. However, no evidence is provided that this form is superior to using the node's embedding alone (i.e., $\alpha=1$). The ablation in Section 4.5 only shows degradation at low $\alpha$, not superiority over $\alpha=1$. In graphs with weak community signals or high heterogeneity, averaging neighbor information could actually harm performance by forcing homogeneity. This design choice needs justification through comparison to simpler variants.

- Overstated novelty relative to vGraph: The paper positions VECODER as fundamentally different from vGraph due to learning a single embedding. However, both models use variational inference, community embeddings, and similar ELBO objectives. The key distinction—single vs. dual embeddings—is not shown to be the source of performance gains. In fact, vGraph’s dual embeddings serve distinct roles (content vs. context), which may be more expressive. By attributing improvements solely to architectural simplification without ablation, the paper overstates its methodological novelty and risks misattributing gains to the wrong factor.

- Evaluation conflates tasks and omits key baselines: Node classification results are reported on citation datasets, but many baselines (e.g., ComE, vGraph) are not designed or tuned for semi-supervised node classification. More critically, the paper omits standard strong baselines like GraphSAGE or GCN for node classification, instead comparing to older methods like DeepWalk. For community detection, while many baselines are included, the comparison does not isolate the effect of the generative framework—e.g., a non-variational version of the same model would help determine whether gains are due to structure or inference. This makes it hard to assess true contribution.

- Scalability claims lack large-graph validation: The paper claims high scalability and reports training times on mid-sized graphs (up to ~20K edges), but the largest dataset (Cora-full) has only 126K edges—well within reach of many GNNs. No experiments are conducted on graphs with millions of edges or nodes, where $\mathcal{O}(|\mathcal{E}|K + NKd)$ complexity could become prohibitive. Without such evaluation, the claim of superior scalability remains unverified, especially given that methods like DGI already scale inductively to large graphs.

- No ablation isolating joint learning benefit: The paper claims that community detection and node representation mutually improve through joint learning, but provides no ablation study that disables one component to assess its impact on the other. For example, fixing community assignments during training (e.g., using random or precomputed clusters) would show whether embedding quality degrades, and vice versa. Without such experiments, the claimed synergy remains speculative. To validate the joint framework, the authors should compare VECODER against a variant where community assignments are held constant during embedding updates, and measure performance drop in node classification and community detection on datasets like Cora and Amazon. This is standard practice in multi-task GNN papers and necessary to support the core argument.

- Decoder design lacks comparison to standard alternatives: The edge decoder in Equation (12) uses a non-standard averaging of sigmoid-transformed dot products involving community embeddings, but no justification is given for why this outperforms a simple inner product between node embeddings. Readers will wonder whether the performance gain comes from the community-aware structure or just the added complexity. The authors should compare VECODER’s decoder against a baseline version that uses $\sigma(\bm{z}_i^\top \bm{z}_j)$ as in VGAE, keeping all other components fixed. Ablating this on Cora and CiteSeer would clarify the contribution of the proposed decoder. Without this, the design choice appears arbitrary and undermines confidence in the model’s inductive bias.

- No evaluation on graphs with strong vs. weak community structure: The model assumes that connected nodes share community assignments, but this may fail in graphs with weak or hierarchical community signals. The paper evaluates only on datasets with ground truth communities, but does not test whether VECODER’s performance degrades when this assumption is violated. A natural check is to evaluate on a synthetic graph where community structure is systematically weakened—e.g., a stochastic block model with decreasing inter-community edge probability. The authors should generate such a series and plot VECODER’s NMI and classification F1 as a function of community strength, comparing to DGI and DeepWalk. This would demonstrate the method’s robustness and clarify its applicability beyond strong-clustering regimes.

- Complexity claim unverified on large-scale graphs: The paper claims $\mathcal{O}(|\mathcal{E}|K + NKd)$ complexity and superior scalability, but all experiments are on graphs with fewer than 25K edges. On larger graphs—e.g., Reddit or OGB datasets—this cost could become prohibitive due to the $NK$ term. To substantiate scalability, the authors should report training time and memory usage on at least one graph with over 500K edges, such as ogbn-arxiv or Reddit. Comparing wall-clock time and GPU memory against DGI and vGraph under the same conditions would validate the efficiency claim. Without this, the argument for scalability rests solely on asymptotic analysis, which may not reflect real-world performance.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper presents a well-motivated model with strong empirical results, but the claims of novelty and mutual benefit from joint learning are not sufficiently supported by ablation studies or comparisons. Key components like the decoder and community posterior need justification beyond end-to-end performance. Without these, the contribution risks being perceived as incremental rather than foundational.

Revision targets:

- Add ablation studies removing or freezing the community detection component to isolate its impact on node classification and vice versa.

- Justify the decoder design (Eq. 12) by comparing against a standard inner-product decoder within the same framework.

- Provide evidence that the neighborhood-weighted community posterior (Eq. 11) outperforms a node-only version, ideally across different graph types.

- Clarify the novelty relative to vGraph by isolating the effect of single vs. dual embeddings through controlled experiments.

- Include standard strong baselines for node classification (e.g., GCN, GraphSAGE) and test scalability on larger graphs (e.g., >1M edges).

comments.title: Decoder asymmetry violates undirected graph assumption
comments.quote:
dge decoder as:

$p_{\theta}(a_{ij}|c_{i}=\ell,c_{j}=m,\bm{z}_{i},\bm{z}_{j})=\frac{\sigma(<\bm{z}_{i},\bm{g}_{m}>)+\sigma(<\bm{z}_{j},\bm{g}_{\ell}>)}{2}.$ (12)

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

For better reconstructing the edges, Eq. (12) makes use of the community embeddings, node embeddings and community assignment information simultaneously.
comments.explanation: The edge likelihood in Equation (12) is asymmetric: it uses $\sigma(\langle \bm{z}_i, \bm{g}_m \rangle)$ and $\sigma(\langle \bm{z}_j, \bm{g}_\ell \rangle)$, where $\ell$ and $m$ are the community assignments of nodes $i$ and $j$. For an undirected graph, $a_{ij} = a_{ji}$, so the model should assign the same probability to both directions. However, unless $\ell = m$ and $\langle \bm{z}_i, \bm{g}_m \rangle = \langle \bm{z}_j, \bm{g}_\ell \rangle$, we have $p(a_{ij}) \neq p(a_{ji})$. This violates the symmetry of the adjacency matrix. While averaging may reduce the discrepancy, it does not eliminate it in general. A symmetric formulation—such as using shared communities or symmetric functions of node-community interactions—would preserve consistency with undirected graphs.

comments.title: Posterior uses latent embeddings, violating variational inference principles
comments.quote:
The corresponding approximate posterior $q_{\phi}(c_{i}=k|\bm{z}_{i},\mathcal{I})$ in Eq. (6) is affected by the node embedding $\bm{z}_{i}$ as well as the neighborhood. To design this, our intuition is to consider the similarity of $\bm{g}_{k}$ with the embedding $\bm{z}_{i}$ as well as with the embeddings of the neighbors of the $i$-th node. The overall similarity with neighbors is mathematically formulated as the average of the dot products of their embeddings. Afterwards a hyperparameter $\alpha$ is introduced to control the bias between the effect of $\bm{z}_{i}$ and the set $\mathcal{N}_{i}$ of the neighbors of the $i$-th node. Finally, a softmax is applied as follows

$q_{\phi}(c_{i}=k|\bm{z}_{i},\mathcal{G})=\frac{\text{exp}\Big{(}\alpha<\bm{z}_{i},\bm{g}_{k}>+(1-\alpha)\frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_{i}|}\sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}_{i}}<\bm{z}_{j},\bm{g}_{k}>\Big{)}}{\sum_{\ell=1}^{K}\text{exp}\Big{(}\alpha<\bm{z}_{i},\bm{g}_{\ell}>+(1-\alpha)\frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_{i}|}\sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}_{i}}<\bm{z}_{j},\bm{g}_{\ell}>\Big{)}}.
comments.explanation: The approximate posterior $q_\phi(c_i|\bm{z}_i, \mathcal{G})$ depends on $\bm{z}_j$ for $j \in \mathcal{N}_i$, which are latent node embeddings produced by the encoder. In variational inference, the inference model must depend only on observed data $\mathcal{I}$ and deterministic functions thereof. Using stochastic latent variables $\bm{z}_j$ in the inference path for $c_i$ creates a circular dependency: $\bm{z}_j$ depends on global parameters and structure, yet they are used to infer $c_i$, which affects the ELBO. This violates the conditional independence assumptions of amortized inference. The correct approach would be to use raw neighbor features or fixed aggregates (e.g., mean of input features) instead of $\bm{z}_j$.

comments.title: Mismatch between generative and inference model structure
comments.quote:
For parameterizing $p_{\theta}(c_{i}|\bm{z}_{i})$ in Eq. (4), we introduce community embeddings $\{\bm{g}_{1},\cdots,\bm{g}_{K}\}$: $\bm{g}_{k}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}$. The distribution $p_{\theta}(c_{i}|\bm{z}_{i})$ is then modelled as the softmax of dot products of $\bm{z}_{i}$ with $\bm{g}_{k}$, i.e.

$p_{\theta}(c_{i}=k|\bm{z}_{i})=\frac{\text{exp}(<\bm{z}_{i},\bm{g}_{k}>)}{\sum_{\ell=1}^{K}\text{exp}(<\bm{z}_{i},\bm{g}_{\ell}>)}.$ (10)

The corresponding approximate posterior $q_{\phi}(c_{i}=k|\bm{z}_{i},\mathcal{I})$ in Eq. (6) is affected by the node embedding $\bm{z}_{i}$ as well as the
comments.explanation: The generative model defines $p_\theta(c_i|\bm{z}_i)$ as depending only on $\bm{z}_i$, implying that community assignment is conditionally independent of graph structure given the embedding. However, the inference model $q_\phi(c_i|\bm{z}_i, \mathcal{G})$ explicitly depends on neighborhood structure through $\mathcal{N}_i$ and $\bm{z}_j$, which encodes topological information. This creates a structural mismatch: the prior assumes $c_i \perp \mathcal{G} \mid \bm{z}_i$, but the posterior leverages $\mathcal{G}$ directly. In variational inference, the KL divergence acts as a regularizer only when both distributions are conditioned on the same information. Here, $q$ conditions on more than $p$, making the ELBO inconsistent and potentially leading to under-regularization.

comments.title: Missing non-edge terms in ELBO undermines reconstruction objective
comments.quote: +\sum_{(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}}\mathbb{E}_{(\bm{z}_{i},\bm{z}_{j},c_{i},c_{j})\sim q_{\phi}(\bm{z}_{i},\bm{z}_{j},c_{i},c_{j}|\mathcal{I})}\bigg{\{}\text{log}\bigg{(}p_{\theta}(a_{ij}|c_{i},c_{j},\bm{z}_{i},\bm{z}_{j})\bigg{)}\bigg{\}},
comments.explanation: The third term in Equation (7) sums only over edges $(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}$, but omits non-edges $(i,j)\notin\mathcal{E}$. In a Bernoulli edge model, the full data likelihood requires both presence and absence terms: $\log p_\theta(a_{ij}|\cdot)$ for all $i,j$. Omitting non-edge terms leads to a biased ELBO that ignores negative evidence, encouraging overfitting to observed edges and poor generalization to missing links. This is especially problematic in sparse graphs where most pairs are non-edges. The current formulation fails to model the full adjacency matrix, undermining the generative claim.

comments.title: Incorrect interpretation of hyperparameter α in community posterior
comments.quote: When $\epsilon$ is fixed and $\alpha$ is changed, the results are mostly consistent except when $\alpha$ is set to a low value. Eq. (11) shows that in such a case the node itself is almost neglected and VECoDeR tends to assign communities based upon neighborhood only, which may cause a decrease in the performance. This effect is most visible in amazon1000 dataset because it has only 1.54 points on average per community i.e. there is a good chance for neighbours of a point of being in different communities. Therefore, only depending upon the neighbors will most likely result in poor results.
comments.explanation: The explanation misassigns the role of $\alpha$: when $\alpha \to 0$, the model emphasizes the node’s own embedding similarity $\langle \bm{z}_i, \bm{g}_k \rangle$, not the neighborhood. Conversely, when $\alpha \to 1$, it emphasizes the average neighbor similarity. The text incorrectly claims that low $\alpha$ causes the node to be 'neglected'—in fact, it is high $\alpha$ that downweights the node’s own embedding. This reversal undermines the interpretability of the ablation study. The performance drop at high $\alpha$ may indeed stem from averaging over heterogeneous neighbors in sparse communities, but the causal explanation must reflect the correct dependence on $\alpha$.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about average community size in amazon1000
comments.quote: This effect is most visible in amazon1000 dataset because it has only 1.54 points on average per community i.e. there is a good chance for neighbours of a point of being in different communities.
comments.explanation: The claim that amazon1000 has '1.54 points on average per community' is not supported by any data, citation, or calculation. With $N=1540$ nodes, this would imply approximately 1000 communities—each with fewer than two nodes on average. Such a fine-grained partition is implausible for a real-world co-purchase network and inconsistent with typical community detection benchmarks. If true, it would make community assignment nearly trivial, yet the paper reports non-trivial performance variation. Without evidence from ground truth statistics, this number appears fabricated or miscalculated. Either provide a source for this statistic or remove it.

comments.title: Training iterations mismatch invalidates speed comparison
comments.quote: For vGraph, we train for 1000 iterations and for VECODER for 1500 iterations. For all other algorithms we use the default parameters as used in section 4.3.
comments.explanation: The comparison of training times is invalid because VECODER is trained for 1.5 times more iterations than vGraph, yet the paper claims it is 'about 40 times faster' on Cora-full. This discrepancy alone could account for much of the reported speed difference. A fair comparison requires equal convergence criteria—either the same number of iterations or evidence that both models have converged (e.g., stabilized loss). Without this, the claim of superior efficiency is misleading. Report time per iteration or use matched stopping criteria to isolate computational cost from convergence behavior.

comments.title: Edge decoder complexity claim omits dot product cost
comments.quote: Eq. (12) takes  $\mathcal{O}(|\mathcal{E}|)$  time for all edges as we have already calculated the dot products.
comments.explanation: The claim that Equation (12) takes $\mathcal{O}(|\mathcal{E}|)$ time assumes that node-node and community-community dot products are precomputed or free. However, $\sigma(\langle \bm{z}_i, \bm{z}_j \rangle)$ requires $\mathcal{O}(d)$ per edge, totaling $\mathcal{O}(|\mathcal{E}|d)$. Similarly, $\sigma(\langle \bm{g}_{c_i}, \bm{g}_{c_j} \rangle)$ requires $\mathcal{O}(d)$ per edge unless community embeddings are scalar. The $\mathcal{O}(NKd)$ term accounts for node-community dot products, not node-node. Thus, the total cost for the decoder is $\mathcal{O}(|\mathcal{E}|d)$, not $\mathcal{O}(|\mathcal{E}|)$. The final complexity should be revised to $\mathcal{O}(|\mathcal{E}|(K + d) + NKd)$ to reflect this.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of vGraph's embedding architecture
comments.quote: CNRL, ComE and vGraph require learning two embeddings for each node for simultaneously tackling the two tasks. Unlike them, VECoDeR learns a single community-aware node representation which is directly used for both tasks.
comments.explanation: The claim that vGraph learns 'two embeddings for each node' is incorrect. According to Sun et al. (2019), vGraph learns one node embedding per node and separate community-level embeddings—not two distinct node embeddings. The node representation is shared across tasks and used for both reconstruction and community inference. By misrepresenting vGraph as using dual node embeddings, the paper exaggerates the novelty of its own single-embedding design. This undermines the central claim of architectural simplification. Accurately describe vGraph’s design to avoid implying a false contrast.

comments.title: Unjustified i.i.d. assumption for community assignments
comments.quote:
Furthermore, assuming $c_{i}|\bm{z}_{i}$ to be $i.i.d$ random variables, the joint distributions in (2) can be factorized as

$p(\bm{Z})=\prod_{i=1}^{N}p(\bm{z}_{i})$ (3)
$p_{\theta}(\bm{c}|\bm{Z})=\prod_{i=1}^{N}p_{\theta}(c_{i}|\bm{z}_{i})$ (4)
$p_{\theta}
comments.explanation: The assumption that $c_i | \bm{z}_i$ are i.i.d. enables the factorization in Equation (4), but it contradicts the goal of modeling overlapping communities, which are inherently correlated across nodes. Connected nodes are expected to share community memberships, yet the generative model imposes conditional independence of $c_i$ given $\bm{z}_i$, ruling out any direct dependency between $c_i$ and $c_j$ even when $i$ and $j$ are neighbors. This disconnect means the generative model cannot produce realistic community-structured graphs from the prior. A more coherent approach would introduce dependencies in $p_\theta(\bm{c}|\bm{Z})$, for example via a Markov random field or pairwise interactions.

comments.title: Thresholding rule forces non-empty community assignments
comments.quote:
To get overlapping community assignments for  $i$ -th node, we can threshold its weighted probability vector at  $\epsilon$ , a hyperparameter, as follows

$$
\mathcal {C} _ {i} = \left\{k \left| \frac {q _ {\phi} \left(c _ {i} = k \mid z _ {i} , \mathcal {I}\right)}{\max  _ {\ell} q _ {\phi} \left(c _ {i} = \ell \mid z _ {i} , \mathcal {I}\right)} \geq \epsilon \right. \right\}, \quad \epsilon \in [ 0, 1 ]. \tag {14}
$$
comments.explanation: The rule in Equation (14) guarantees that $\mathcal{C}_i$ is non-empty for all nodes because the maximizing community $k^*$ satisfies $q_\phi(c_i=k^*) / \max_\ell q_\phi = 1 \geq \epsilon$. This forces every node to belong to at least one community, even if all probabilities are low and the node is structurally isolated. In overlapping community detection, it is valid for a node to belong to no community with sufficient confidence. The model provides no mechanism to represent such cases. A thresholding rule should allow $\mathcal{C}_i = \emptyset$ when no $q_\phi(c_i=k)$ exceeds a meaningful level.

comments.title: Normalization in thresholding distorts hyperparameter interpretation
comments.quote:
To get overlapping community assignments for  $i$ -th node, we can threshold its weighted probability vector at  $\epsilon$ , a hyperparameter, as follows

$$
\mathcal {C} _ {i} = \left\{k \left| \frac {q _ {\phi} \left(c _ {i} = k \mid z _ {i} , \mathcal {I}\right)}{\max  _ {\ell} q _ {\phi} \left(c _ {i} = \ell \mid z _ {i} , \mathcal {I}\right)} \geq \epsilon \right. \right\}, \quad \epsilon \in [ 0, 1 ]. \tag {14}
$$
comments.explanation: The normalization by the maximum probability creates a relative threshold that depends on the strongest community assignment. This means that for a node with all low-probability memberships, even a very small absolute probability may be selected if it is close to the maximum. Conversely, a node with one dominant community and several moderate ones may exclude those moderate communities even if their absolute probabilities are meaningful. This inconsistency makes $\epsilon$ dataset- and node-dependent. A more standard and interpretable approach is to use absolute thresholds: $q_\phi(c_i=k) \geq \epsilon'$.

comments.title: Ambiguous dataset naming and construction for Amazon subsets
comments.quote:
Moreover, we also analyse the case of large number of communities. For this purpose, we prepare two subsets

of amazon dataset by randomly selecting 500 and 1000 communities from 2000 smallest communities in the amazon dataset.
comments.explanation: The text states that only the 5 largest communities are used for standard Amazon evaluation, yet refers to a version with at least 2000 communities. This implies the base Amazon dataset contains many more than 5 communities—only that 5 are used for standard evaluation. But the paper does not clarify this, leading readers to assume the Amazon dataset has only 5 communities, making the construction of amazon500 impossible. The paper must explicitly state that the underlying Amazon graph has many ground-truth communities, from which different subsets are extracted for evaluation.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about joint learning superiority
comments.quote: Third, it is noted that all the methods which achieve second best performance are solving the task of community detection and node representation learning jointly. This supports our claim that treating the two tasks jointly results in better performance.
comments.explanation: The assertion that second-best performers all use joint learning is factually incorrect. In Table 2, MNMF achieves second-best F1 on fb0 and is a pure community detection method without node representation learning. Similarly, Circles achieves second-best on fb1912 and fb3437 and has no learned node embeddings. These are not joint frameworks. Therefore, the evidence cited directly contradicts the claim. The argument cannot support the central thesis of joint learning benefits if the top competitors include non-joint methods.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces VECoDeR, a variational approach for joint community detection and node representation learning. By using a single embedding space and a decoder that incorporates community structure, the authors aim to improve computational efficiency and performance on downstream tasks.

The paper presents a clear and computationally efficient framework that addresses the joint task of community detection and node representation. The empirical results show consistent improvements over several baselines. However, the theoretical justification for the decoder design and the handling of graph sparsity require further clarification.

Overview issues:

- Unjustified Decoder Design: The edge decoder in Equation (12) is defined as the average of two sigmoid-transformed dot products. While this heuristic combines node and community embeddings, it lacks a clear probabilistic derivation from the generative model defined in Section 3.2. Specifically, it is unclear how this specific functional form relates to the likelihood p(a_ij|c_i, c_j, z_i, z_j). The authors should provide a derivation or at least a stronger motivation for why this specific average of sigmoid functions is appropriate for capturing edge probability in a community-aware context.

- Handling of Graph Sparsity: The model relies on the neighborhood information in Equation (11) to learn community assignments. In extremely sparse graphs, the neighborhood N_i may be very small or empty, which could lead to high variance in the estimation of the posterior q(c_i|z_i, G). The paper does not address how the model behaves for isolated nodes or nodes with very low degrees. Including an analysis of performance across varying node degrees would clarify the robustness of the variational posterior estimation.

- Lack of Model Selection for K: The methodology assumes a fixed number of communities K throughout the experiments. In real-world applications, K is rarely known a priori. The paper fails to provide a mechanism for automatic community discovery or model selection, which limits the practical utility of the proposed framework. Adding an analysis of how the model performs under misspecified K or proposing a non-parametric extension would significantly strengthen the contribution.

- Independence Assumption in Posterior: Equation (8) assumes that the joint posterior q(z_i, z_j, c_i, c_j|I) factorizes into independent distributions for nodes i and j. This assumption ignores the inherent dependencies between connected nodes in the latent space, which is exactly what the model intends to capture. While this simplifies computation, the authors should discuss the impact of this mean-field-like approximation on the quality of the learned representations.

- Empirical validation of the generative model's edge reconstruction: The paper claims that VECoDeR is a generative model, yet it does not demonstrate the model's ability to generate synthetic graphs that resemble the target real-world networks. A standard check for generative graph models is to sample from the learned distribution p(A|c, Z) and compare the degree distribution, clustering coefficient, or community structure of the synthetic graphs to the original datasets. Without this, it is unclear if the model truly captures the generative process or if it merely acts as a discriminative embedding learner. I suggest adding a section that visualizes or quantitatively compares the statistics of graphs generated by VECoDeR against the ground-truth graphs for a representative dataset like Cora or Facebook.

- Missing ablation study on the shared decoder architecture: A primary contribution of the paper is the use of a single node embedding for both representation and community context, which the authors argue is sufficient. However, the paper lacks an ablation study that compares this 'shared' approach against a 'dual-embedding' baseline (e.g., one where the node representation and community context are learned as separate vectors but trained with the same VECoDeR objective). This is necessary to isolate whether the performance gains stem from the joint learning framework itself or specifically from the reduction to a single embedding. Please include an experiment comparing VECoDeR against a variant where the node embedding and context embedding are decoupled to verify the claim that a single embedding is sufficient.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper provides a compelling and efficient method, but the theoretical grounding of the decoder and the robustness of the model to graph sparsity need more rigorous treatment. The lack of discussion on model selection for the number of communities also limits the paper's impact in practical settings.

Revision targets:

- Provide a formal derivation or more rigorous justification for the edge decoder in Equation (12).

- Include an empirical analysis of model performance specifically for low-degree or isolated nodes to address sparsity concerns.

- Discuss or implement a strategy for automatic selection of the number of communities K.

- Clarify the implications of the independence assumption in the posterior factorization in Equation (8).

comments.title: Inconsistent iteration counts for training time comparison
comments.quote: For vGraph, we train for 1000 iterations and for VECODER for 1500 iterations. For all other algorithms we use the default parameters as used in section 4.3.
comments.explanation: The comparison of training times is biased because the models are trained for different numbers of iterations. To claim VECoDeR is significantly faster than vGraph, you must compare the time required to reach a comparable level of convergence or performance, rather than using arbitrary, unequal iteration counts. Please clarify if these counts were chosen to reach convergence for each model and provide convergence metrics to justify the comparison.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of vGraph's generative process
comments.quote: For a given node, vGraph, first draws a community assignment and then a connected neighbor node is generated based on the assignment. Whereas, VECoDeR draws the node embedding from prior distribution and then community assignment is conditioned on a single node only. In simple terms, vGraph also needs edge information in the generative process whereas VECoDeR does not require it.
comments.explanation: The claim that vGraph requires edge information in the generative process while VECoDeR does not is misleading. vGraph is a generative model for graphs, so it naturally incorporates edge information in its likelihood function. VECoDeR also relies on edge reconstruction, as evidenced by the decoder in Equation (12), to learn embeddings. Suggesting that VECoDeR avoids edge information in its generative process contradicts the fundamental requirement of a graph autoencoder to reconstruct the adjacency matrix.

comments.title: Inconsistency in dataset statistics for Cora-full
comments.explanation: The table lists the number of features |F| for Cora-full as 8710. However, standard benchmarks for this dataset typically report 8710 as the number of nodes, not the number of features. Given that the node count |V| is listed as 19793, it is likely that the values for |V| and |F| have been swapped or misattributed. Please verify these statistics against the original source to ensure the model is evaluated on the correct feature dimensionality.

comments.title: Inconsistent performance claims for VECoDeR
comments.quote: VECoDeR also outperforms its competitors on 12 out of 13 datasets in terms of F1-score. It is the second best method on the 13th dataset (fb0).
comments.explanation: The claim that VECoDeR is the second best on the fb0 dataset for F1-score is not supported by Table 2. In the fb0 row, the F1-score for VECoDeR is 34.7, while ComGan achieves 35.0 and ComE achieves 31.1. This makes VECoDeR the second best, but the claim that it outperforms competitors on 12 out of 13 datasets is inaccurate because it is not the top performer on fb0. Please revise the text to accurately reflect the ranking shown in the table.

comments.title: Ambiguity in latent variable representation for overlapping communities
comments.quote: Given $K$ as the number of communities, we aim to jointly learn the node embeddings and the community embeddings following a variational approach such that: (1) One or more communities can be assigned to every node
comments.explanation: The requirement for overlapping community membership is not reflected in the latent variable space definition. If c_i is a categorical variable, it restricts the node to a single community. To support overlapping memberships, c_i must be defined as a vector in {0, 1}^K or [0, 1]^K representing membership strengths. Without this formal definition, the variational objective function remains underspecified. Please add a formal definition of the latent variable c_i to specify the support of the community membership distribution.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for node latent variables
comments.quote: t overlapping community assignments for  $i$ -th node, we can threshold its weighted probability vector at  $\epsilon$ , a hyperparameter, as follows
comments.explanation: The notation for the latent variable is inconsistent. Equation (13) uses the bolded vector notation z_i, while Equation (14) uses the unbolded z_i. This creates ambiguity as to whether z_i represents a scalar component or the full vector. Please standardize the notation to z_i in Equation (14) to maintain consistency with the rest of the paper.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes VECoDeR, a variational generative model for joint community detection and node representation learning. The main strengths are the unified single-embedding framework, extensive experiments on 18 datasets, and strong empirical results across three tasks. However, several methodological and evaluative issues need attention.

Overview issues:

- Missing derivation and justification of the ELBO: The ELBO in Eq. (7) is presented without derivation, and its form raises questions. The second term uses a Monte Carlo average over M samples of the KL divergence between the approximate posterior and prior for community assignments, but this is not standard—typically the KL is computed analytically when both distributions are known. The paper states that M=1 suffices but does not justify this choice or analyze the variance of the estimator. Moreover, the ELBO appears to treat the community assignments as latent variables in the variational framework, yet the generative model in Eq. (2) conditions edges on both Z and c, while the inference model factorizes as q(Z,c|I) = q(Z|I) q(c|Z,I). The derivation should be provided in full, and the assumptions underlying the factorization and the KL terms should be explicitly stated. Without this, readers cannot assess whether the objective function correctly lower-bounds the log-marginal likelihood.

- Unclear handling of overlapping communities in the generative model: The generative model assumes each node has a single community assignment c_i, as seen in Eq. (4) where p(c_i|z_i) is a categorical distribution. However, the paper claims to handle overlapping communities, and the inference procedure in Eq. (14) assigns multiple communities per node by thresholding.
